input.headline: I think the language is really cool - London residents attend Irish language workshop

raw.headline: I think the language is really cool - London residents attend Irish language workshop

winner: I attended an Irish language workshop where we learned how to say "London" in Gaelic—turns out it's "Londain." The instructor said it was crucial for pronunciation, but honestly, I just kept imagining a tiny island of London floating in my mouth.

candidates.text: I tried attending an Irish language workshop in London, and now I can say "goodbye" in three different accents.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 8.5, "reason": "Surprising twist on language learning, concise, and ties well with the headline's context."}

candidates.text: I attended an Irish language workshop where we learned how to say "London" in Gaelic—turns out it's "Londain." The instructor said it was crucial for pronunciation, but honestly, I just kept imagining a tiny island of London floating in my mouth.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 8.5, "reason": "Surprising twist with the Gaelic pronunciation, specific reference to London, and a clever metaphor about imagining an island in one's mouth."}

candidates.text: I thought the language was really cool—until I realized the only thing being translated was my will to live.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 8.5, "reason": "Surprising twist on the headline with dark humor and clever wordplay."}

candidates.text: Why did the Irish language workshop in London make me crave a pint? Because it was so *Gaeilge* for my taste!
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 6.5, "reason": "The joke uses a play on words with 'Gaeilge' (the Irish language) and 'pint' (a beer), which is somewhat clever but lacks full clarity and impact. The surprise element is mild, and the specificity could be stronger."}

candidates.text: I told my friend I was going to an Irish language workshop, and he said, "You're learning Gaelic?" I replied, "No, I'm just trying to sound like a Londoner who thinks the language is really cool.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 6.5, "reason": "The joke is somewhat clever with the twist about sounding like a Londoner, but it's a bit long and the humor relies on a mild pun that may not land as strongly for all audiences."}

candidates.text: Why did the Londoner bring a dictionary to his Irish workshop? To look up the meaning of "not speaking English.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 6.5, "reason": "The joke is somewhat surprising and specific to the headline, but the punchline is a bit forced and lacks refinement. The setup could be more concise, and the humor feels slightly generic."}

candidates.text: London residents are learning Irish because they want to spice up their vocabulary, but they're finding it hard to keep their cool when they realize the language is actually quite " Gael-ic" to learn.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 7.5, "reason": "The joke is clever with the pun on 'Gael-ic' and the setup about spicing up vocabulary. It's specific to the headline and concise, though the humor could be more surprising."}

candidates.text: London residents attended an Irish language workshop to learn how to speak it, but they were disappointed to find out that the language isn't actually cool to the touch.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 6.5, "reason": "The joke is somewhat surprising and specific, but the humor relies on a forced pun ('cool to the touch') that feels a bit contrived and doesn't fully align with the headline's context."}

candidates.text: London residents attended an Irish language workshop today because they were tired of the local dialect being too easy to understand.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 6.5, "reason": "The joke is somewhat surprising and specific, but the humor is weak due to a forced logic (tired of dialect being too easy) that doesn't strongly connect to the headline."}
